title: How ecosystems rebuild and transform over time

lesson.learn:
Ecological succession is the process by which a community changes after disturbance or the creation of new land. Pioneer species arrive first, soil begins to build, and a more complex community gradually follows. What starts as bare rock, ash, or empty ground can slowly become forest, grassland, or another mature ecosystem.

Succession matters because ecosystems are not fixed snapshots. They are living processes that move through stages as species arrive, compete, die, and create conditions for what comes next.
lesson.simpler: Succession is nature's way of rebuilding an ecosystem step by step after a disturbance or on fresh land.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Succession changes environments by replacing simple early communities with more complex later ones.
lesson.deeperDive:
Primary succession begins where there is no soil at all, such as lava flows or land exposed by retreating glaciers. Secondary succession starts where soil remains after a fire, storm, or human disturbance. In both cases, the process usually moves through grasses, shrubs, young trees, and eventually a more stable community.

The exact endpoint depends on climate, soil, and disturbance. Succession is not a march toward one perfect finish line. It is a response to conditions that keeps ecosystems dynamic over time.
lesson.funFact: A forest can begin on bare rock if the conditions are right.

places: Lava fields - primary succession starts here
Burned forests - secondary succession is common
Glacier forelands - newly exposed land changes quickly
years: 1900s - succession becomes a formal ecology concept
concepts: Pioneer species - first organisms to arrive
Secondary succession - starts where soil remains
Community assembly - the building of an ecosystem over time
